mnemonics.title: LRN - Left Right Node
mnemonics.content: LEFT subtree first, RIGHT subtree second, NODE (root) last. REMEMBER: 'LEARN' stands for Left, Explore, Answer, Right, eNcode - Postorder.

mnemonics.title: NLR - Node Left Right
mnemonics.content: NODE first, then LEFT subtree, then RIGHT subtree. PRE-order means visit ROOT BEFORE children. Think: 'N' comes before 'L' and 'R' in alphabet.

mnemonics.title: LNR - Left Node Right
mnemonics.content: LEFT subtree, then NODE, then RIGHT. IN-order means INSERT in between. Think: Root goes IN the middle of its subtrees.

acronyms.usage: Produces sorted output for BST, visits left subtree, then root, then right subtree.
Used for copying trees, prefix expressions; visits root before children.
Used for deleting trees, postfix expressions; visits root after children.
Explores all nodes at present depth before moving to next depth level.
Explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking.

visualTips.visualization: Draw the tree on paper. For traversal, trace your finger: for inorder, go from bottom-left, up to node, then bottom-right. Imagine painting nodes in sequence.
Think of recursion like a stack of plates. Each recursive call is a plate on top. You must finish (pop) the top plate before finishing the one below.
Imagine a queue at a ticket counter. People (nodes) join at the back and get served in order. Children join the queue as their parent gets served.
Think of a stack of books. You push nodes going down-left, pop to visit, then push right subtrees. LIFO order ensures you finish one branch before starting another.

analogies.analogy: Like reading a book page by page from top to bottom, then moving to the next page. Left subtree is previous page, right subtree is next page, current node is current page.
Like writing your name first, then your parents' names, then grandparents. You document yourself before your ancestors.
Like cleaning a house: clean each room (left subtree) first, then the hallway (right subtree), finally the main entrance (root) last.
Like reading rows in a classroom: read all seats in row 1 (level 1), then all seats in row 2 (level 2), moving left to right.
Like finding your way in a maze without breadcrumbs - you create temporary markers (threads) to find your way back without remembering the path.
